coredata.dc:title: Carbon-quantum-dots-embedded MnO2 nanoflower as an efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution in alkaline media
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Lin
Wang, Jiaojiao
Wang, Kun
Wo, Hengxuan
Wang, Xiang
Zhuang, Wenchang
Li, Tongxiang
Du, Xihua
coredata.dc:description: Although great advances have been achieved in the field of electrocatalysis, highly active, durable, and earth-abundant electrocatalysts for oxygen production are in increasingly demand as a result of their promising application in future energy-conversion technologies. Here, the construction of a novel class of cost-efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER), employing carbon dots (CDs), is reported. Benefitting from the successful incorporation of CDs into MnO2 nanoflowers, the resultant CDs-MnO2 with high surface areas, enhanced conductivity, as well as fast charge transfer pathways display not only greatly promoted electrocatalytic activity but also high stability for OER. Remarkably, to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2, the optimal of CDs0.15-MnO2 requires the overpotential of only 343 mV and the relatively low Tafel slope of 43.6 mV dec−1. More significantly, such CDs0.15-MnO2 catalyst also displays outstanding long-term stability with limited activity and morphology change, showing an advanced class electrocatalyst. These findings open up a new way for the application of CDs and add to the growing family of CDs-metal hybrids with high OER performances.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province
Jiangsu province key R & D projects of China
University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Jiangsu province key R & D projects of China ( BE2016648 ), Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province ( 16KJA210001 ), University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province ( 15KJB150028 ), Dedicated funds for promoting sci-teci nnovation of Xuzhou ( KC17080 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative TEM, SEM images of (a–d) MnO2 nanoflowers and (e and f) CDs with different magnifications. (g) PXRD patterns and (h) infrared spectra of CDs MnO2 nanoflowers, and CDs0.15-MnO2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a and b) Representative TEM images of CDs0.15-MnO2 with different magnifications. (c) HRTEM image, (d) PXRD pattern, as well as (e) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of hierarchical CDs0.15-MnO2. (f) TGA curves of MnO2, CDs, and CDs0.15-MnO2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan, (b) Mn 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) C 1s, and (e) N 1s of CDs0.15-MnO2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (a and b) CDs0.1-MnO2, (c and d) CDs0.2-MnO2, and (e and f) CDs0.25-MnO2 with different magnifications. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

OER electrochemical measurements. (a) LSV Polarization curves, (b) onset potential, (c) Tafel slopes, and (d) overpotentials of CDs0.15-MnO2, CDs0.2-MnO2, CDs0.25-MnO2, MnO2, and CDs0.1-MnO2 at the current density of 10 mA cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Polarization curves of (a) MnO2 and (b) CDs0.15-MnO2 before and after 3000 cycles CVs. (c) Prolonged CP of MnO2 and CDs0.15-MnO2 for 36 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon-quantum-dots-embedded MnO2 nanoflower as an efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution in alkaline media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou, 221111, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, Xuzhou, 221111, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Institute of China University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nano Institute of China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although great advances have been achieved in the field of electrocatalysis, highly active, durable, and earth-abundant electrocatalysts for oxygen production are in increasingly demand as a result of their promising application in future energy-conversion technologies. Here, the construction of a novel class of cost-efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER), employing carbon dots (CDs), is reported. Benefitting from the successful incorporation of CDs into MnO2 nanoflowers, the resultant CDs-MnO2 with high surface areas, enhanced conductivity, as well as fast charge transfer pathways display not only greatly promoted electrocatalytic activity but also high stability for OER. Remarkably, to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2, the optimal of CDs0.15-MnO2 requires the overpotential of only 343 mV and the relatively low Tafel slope of 43.6 mV dec−1. More significantly, such CDs0.15-MnO2 catalyst also displays outstanding long-term stability with limited activity and morphology change, showing an advanced class electrocatalyst. These findings open up a new way for the application of CDs and add to the growing family of CDs-metal hybrids with high OER performances.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid depletion of nonrenewable fossil fuel along with increasing energy demand in people's daily lives made the world face a big energy and environment challenge [1,2]. To solve energy shortage problems and relieve our dependences on traditional fossil fuels, it is urgent to exploit and develop renewable and clean energy sources [3]. Hydrogen, an abundant renewable and clean energy source, is generally regarded as one of the most appropriate substitutes of conventional fossil fuels due to environment-friendly properties, high energy density, as well as easy to make [4,5]. Photochemical and electrochemical water splitting are two generally employed routes to produce hydrogen [6–8]. Generally, owing to the high-efficiency and mass-produced properties, electrochemical water splitting has thus been extensively researched [9]. Unfortunately, due to the four-proton-coupled electron transfer process, the sluggish reaction kinetic for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) has been the biggest obstacle hinders the large-scale commercialization of electrochemical water splitting [10]. To address this issue and fulfill high-efficiency hydrogen production, it is necessary to design and develop highly active OER catalysts. Currently, Ir/Ru-based oxides are employed as benchmark electrocatalysts for OER, while their high prices along with limited reserves have thus impeded their large-scale application. To this end, exploring and developing highly active and earth-abundant electrocatalysts are urgently sought.

At present, various transition metal oxides/hydroxides, such as Co3O4, NiO, Co(OH)2, and Ni(OH)2 have been developed for serving as highly active OER catalysts due to their abundances, environmentally benign nature, as well as inherently high OER activity [11–15]. Apart from these catalysts, manganese oxides, particular for MnO2 have received considerable attentions as an ideal candidate for various electrochemical reactions such as oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), supercacitors, and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) because of their low cost and toxicity but high electrochemical stability and catalytic activity [16–19].

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs), with the features of high conductivity, low cost, large specific surface area, and excellent electron-transfer ability have been employed as efficient electrocatalysts for various reaction [20,21]. Moreover, the CDs containing abundant functional groups such as –OH, -COOH, and –NH2 on their surface can also provide many beneficial sites for the fabrication of multicomponent photo- and electroactive catalysts [22,23]. More importantly, the strong interaction inside these components can also effectively enhance their electrocatalytic performances via boosting the intermolecular electron transfer [24,25]. To this end, if we can combine the superiorities of MnO2 and CDs to construct the novel class of CDs-MnO2 components, it will be favorable for boosting the OER electrocatalysis.

Herein, we reported the synthesis of a novel class of CDs-MnO2 components through a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. And systematic characterizations revealed that the incorporation of CDs was favorable for splitting the big MnO2 nanoflowers into several small nanoflowers with rough surface, and the prepared CDs-MnO2 can work as highly efficient electrocatalysts for OER. Remarkably, the optimal CDs0.15-MnO2 display high performances for OER, which requires the overpotential of only 343 mV to achieve the current density of 10 mA cm−2 and maintain high electrocatalytic and structural stability, showing a promising class of electrocatalysts for OER.

CDs were prepared by a facile microwave pyrolysis method according to the literature report [22]. Experimentally, 5.0 g ammonium citrate and 1.0 g sodium dihydrogen phosphate were accurately weighed and dissolved in a beaker containing 10 mL distilled water. The solid was completely dissolved by sonication for 5–10 min. The solution was then put in a microwave oven and heated for 3 min. When the liquid changed from colorless to yellow or yellow brown solid, we then stopped the reactions. The products were then collected via centrifugation and washed with the mixture of ethanol and water (4:1) for three times when cooled to room temperature naturally. Then the product was poured into a surface dish and dried for 4 h at 80 °C to obtain the yellowish-brown CDs.

The MnO2 nanoflowers were prepared through a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method according to the following reaction: 2KMnO4 + 8HCl = 2KCl + 4H2O + 4MnO2 + 3 Cl2. Generally, 0.45 g KMnO4 were first dissolved into 20 mL distilled water in a beaker. After complete dissolution of KMnO4, 1 mL concentrated HCl was then added slowly to above solution with rapid stirring. After continuous reaction for 10 min at the temperature of 100 °C in microwave reactor and cooled to room temperature naturally, the products were then filtered and washed with ethanol and water for three times. Finally, the black MnO2 were dried at 60 °C.

CDs-MnO2 was also prepared by a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, where 0.15 g CDs and 0.45 g KMnO4 were dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and stirred for 10 min. Meanwhile, 1 mL concentrated HCl (11.74 M) was dissolved in a small beaker and diluted with 20 mL distilled water. Then, the dilute HCl solution was slowly added to above solution under rapid stirring. And the mixture was put into the microwave reactor for 10 min at the temperature was 100 °C. After the reaction, the black solid was obtained by cooling to the safe temperature and filtered, washed three times with ethanol and water.

Scanning electron microscope images were conducted on a scanning electron microscope (SEM) JEOL, JSM-6330FT. The morphologies of samples were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) from JEOL-2100, which operated at 100 kV instruments. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected using an X'Pert-Pro X-ray powder diffractometer equipped with a Cu radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). The scan rate is 10°/min and the step size is 0.02. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra (XPS) were calibrated by conducted C 1s (284.9 eV) on a JEOL JPS-9010 MC spectrometer. Thermogravimetric (TGA) measurements were performed in a Mettler-Toledo TGA instrument. About 10 mg of each sample was placed in an alumina crucible and heated to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in an N2 flow, and the flow rate was 60 mL/min. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas of the products were determined using a nitrogen adsorption analyzer (Quadrasorb-S1, Quantachrome, USA) and the pore-size distribution was estimated by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda method.

For the OER measurement, where the catalysts modified electrodes were employed as working electrodes, a Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and a carbon rod as counter electrode, respectively. To prepare the working electrode, 2 mg samples were first dispersed in a solution (1:9, v/v) containing Nafion (5%) and water (2 mL) by sonication. Then, 20 μL freshly prepared suspension was dropped on the surface of pre-polished glassy carbon electrode (GCE, diameter: 3 mm, area: 0.07 cm2) and dried at room temperature for 12 h before measurements. Therefore, the density of the deposited catalysts is 0.3 mg/cm2. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted in 1 M KOH solution at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in the potential range from 1 to 1.7 V (vs RHE). The polarization curves were acquired using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) for OER in 1.0 M KOH solution at the scanning rate of 5 mV s−1 in the potential range from 1 to 1.7 V (vs RHE). iR drop was compensated at 95% for measurements. The chronopotentiometric (CP) and LSV after continuous 3000 cycle CV were also conducted to evaluate their durability. For comparison, MnO2 nanoflowers were also used as the baseline catalysts. It is worth noting that all the potentials measured were calibrated to reverse hydrogen electrode (RHE).

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the preparation of CDs-MnO2 was carried out through a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal strategy, where freshly prepared CDs and KMnO4 were used as carbon and Mn precursors, respectively. For ease of comparison, both CDs and MnO2 nanoflowers were also prepared and thoroughly characterized. As seen in Fig.1 a and d, the TEM images revealed that the as-obtained MnO2 displayed the typical nanoflower structure with the average size of 400–500 nm. A clear observation in SEM images (Fig. 1c and d) indicated that such MnO2 nanoflower structures were also featured with honeycomb-like properties, which may thus possess abundant surface areas. To investigate the morphological features of CDs, the TEM images with different magnifications have also been obtained.

As seen in Fig. 1e and f, the as-obtained CDs are highly dispersed with the average diameter of around 3.6 nm (Fig.S1). Moreover, we have also performed the PXRD to investigate their crystal structures. As seen in Fig. 1g, a wide PXRD peak located at the 2θ degree of 20° was in good accordance with the PXRD pattern of CDs, indicating the successful construction of amorphous CDs. With regard to the MnO2, as seen, the PXRD peaks at the 2θ degree of 12.340°, 24.799°, 37.371°, and 66.859° are corresponding to the diffraction peaks of K0·27MnO2·0.54H2O (JCPDF#52–0556), suggesting the successful preparation of δ-MnO2 [26]. Furthermore, the infrared spectra of CDs and MnO2 nanoflowers were also obtained and presented in Fig. 1h. As observed, the peaks at the wavenumber of 3300～3550 cm−1 are assigned to the typical stretching peak of –OH and –NH [27], and 1300～1400 cm−1 are attributed to bending vibrated of O-H, and the peak at 1600～1750 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 ∼ 1300 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peak of C=O and C-O, respectively, which is also strong evidence demonstrating the successful synthesis of N-doped CDs [28,29]. As for infrared spectrum of MnO2, where the peaks at 502 cm−1 and 459 cm−1 are ascribed to the adsorption peaks of Mn-O bending vibrations in the [MnO6] octahedron [30,31], The observed bands at approximately 3299 cm−1 and 1616 cm−1 were attributed, respectively, to hydroxyl stretching and bending vibrations of adsorbed solvent molecules, all of which were consistent with the infrared spectrum of δ-MnO2 [30]. The infrared spectrum of CDs/MnO2 was also largely consistent with δ-MnO2. However, the position of some peaks have little differences, due to CDs was embedded into δ-MnO2. The 1616 cm−1 peaks of δ-MnO2.was deconvoluted into two peaks of 1610 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, being assigned to C=O stretching vibrations and O-H bending vibrations [32], respectively. The peak at 1033 cm−1 could be assigned to the O-H bending vibrations [33], and bending vibrations peaks of Mn-O were attributed to 520 and 470 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra of CDs-MnO2 contained signals associated with the peaks of CDs and δ-MnO2, indicating that the successful preparation of CDs0.15-MnO2 composite.

To thoroughly investigate the physiochemical properties of CDs0.15-MnO2, a series of characterizations have been performed. As seen in Fig. 1a and b, the TEM images illustrated that the CDs0.15-MnO2 could also display the typical nanoflower structure with the average size around 200 nm. However, after a clear observation, we found that the incorporation of CDs causes a honeycomb-like nanoflower to be splitted into several smaller MnO2 nanoflowers, and even makes the honeycomb-like structure completely collapses. This changes the state of MnO2 from original tight clusters to more dispersed, with a larger specific surface area and pore size, which is beneficial for enhancing its dispersibility and adsorption capacity in water. To investigate their crystal structures, the HRTEM and PXRD have also been performed. As seen in Fig. 2 c, the lattice spacings were calculated to be 0.34 and 0.27 nm, which were corresponding to the (002) facets of CDs and (411) facets of MnO2 [34], respectively, indicating the successful fabrication of CDs supported MnO2 nanoflowers. The PXRD test has also been performed to further investigate the crystal structure of CDs0.15-MnO2, as observed in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 2θ degree of 37.371° and 66.859° are ascribed to the δ-MnO2, while the typical broad peak at 2θ degree of 20.9° is associated with the graphitic structure of CDs [35]. Interestingly, the PXRD peaks of CDs0.15-MnO2 are much wider than those of δ-MnO2, this is due to the poor crystalline of CDs and δ-MnO2 [36]. Remarkably, the peaks of CDs0.15-MnO2 at the 2θ degree of 12.340° and 24.799° are much weaker than those of δ-MnO2, further confirming the successful construction of CDs0.15-MnO2. The EDS elemental mapping results clearly reveal that the elements of Mn, O, and C are uniformly distributed throughout the CDs0.15-MnO2, suggesting the successful incorporation of CDs into MnO2 (Fig.S2). In consideration of high porosity of such CDs0.15-MnO2 composites, we have performed the BET test to evaluate their porous properties. As seen in Fig.2e and Fig.S3a, b, the BET specific surface area of CDs0.15-MnO2 is 254.83 m2 g−1, which is much larger than that of MnO2 nanoflowers (133.85 m2 g−1), indicating the incorporation of CDs is favorable for enhancing the surface areas of catalysts, being beneficial to exposing more surface active sites to oxygen-containing intermediates. Besides, the thermogravimetric curves (TGA) has also been obtained to study the content of CDs and MnO2. As shown in Fig. 2f, the weight loss of CDs is as high as 70%, which indicates that the carbon skeleton is completely disintegrated at high temperatures. The weight loss of MnO2 is about 20% and the weight loss in the range of 30–280 °C is due to the loss of water molecules in the MnO2 nano-layer. With regard to the CDs0.15-MnO2, the weight loss at the temperature from 30 to 280 °C is mainly ascribed to the loss of water molecules in the CDs0.15-MnO2. The weight loss between 280 and 380 °C is about 10%, which is mainly due to the dehydration of chemical bond of CDs in the composite, the collapse of the composite material skeleton, as well as the collapse of the carbon skeleton. However, the CDs0.15-MnO2 composites show no weight loss between 380 and 800 °C, indicating that only MnO2 is left at this time and the high stability of MnO2.

In order to study the surface compositions of CDs0.15-MnO2, the XPS has also been performed. As seen in Fig. 3 a, the presence of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Mn 2p in the XPS survey scan indicates the successful incorporation of CDs into MnO2 nanoflowers. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p can be well deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3b), where the one at 641.7 eV is associated with the Mn 2p1/2 peak, the other two peaks at 643.8 and 653 eV are ascribed to the Mn (II) 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively, being consistent with the MnO2 XPS spectrum [37]. Fig. 3c shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s, as seen, the O 1s XPS spectrum can be divided into three parts, which are correspondences to the Mn-O (529.6 eV), Mn-O-C (530 eV), and C=O (531.5 eV) groups [38,39]. As for C 1s XPS spectrum, there is a strong signal of C=C at 283.84 eV, a signal of C-O at 285.44 eV, a signal of C=O at 288.04 eV, and C pi- > pi* signal at 292.03 eV (Fig. 3d) [40]. Moreover, the N 1s could be well deconvoluted into two peaks, which were corresponding to graphitic and pyrrolic N (Fig. 3e), respectively [41–43]. From the XPS analyses, we can infer that CDs and MnO2 form a complex in the form of Mn-O-C, thereby producing a CDs0.15-MnO2 nanocomposite.

For studying the influece of content of CDs on the morphology of CDs-MnO2, a series of CDs-MnO2 with different content of CDs have also been successfully engineered. As seen in Fig. 4 a and b, CDs0.1-MnO2 with the CDs content of 0.1 also shows the typical nanoflowers structure with the average size around 300–400 nm. When increased the content of CDs into 0.2, the CDs0.2-MnO2 displayed the collapsed nanoflower structure. With the increase of content of CDs to 0.25, the collapse of CDs0.25-MnO2 become more severe, where no nanoflower structure can be observed. These analyses revealed the great importance of CDs for determining the final morphologies of CDs-MnO2.

Taking the high surface areas, enhanced conductivity, as well as moderated electronic structure into considerations, such CDs0.15-MnO2 are thus highly expected to display outstanding electrochemical properties. To this end, we here employed the OER in 1 M KOH solution to assess their electrocatalytic properties and LSV tests for all catalysts-modified GCEs were conducted in 1.0 M KOH electrolytes to evaluate their electrocatalytic performances with respect to OER. For comparison, the OER activities of CDs0.1-MnO2, CDs0.2-MnO2, CDs0.25-MnO2, and MnO2 catalysts were also thoroughly investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the optimized CDs0.15-MnO2 shows the best capability and highest electrocatalytic activity towards OER, whose onset potential is as low as 1.34 V, being 0.11, 0.18, 0.20, and 0.21 V lower than those of CDs0.2-MnO2, MnO2, CDs0.25-MnO2, and CDs0.1-MnO2, respectively (Fig. 5b). Moreover, for comparing their OER activities, the overpotentials of different catalysts at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 have also been summarized in Fig. 5d. As seen, the optimal CDs0.15-MnO2 displays the highest OER activity with the overpotential of only 343 mV to reach the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is 50, 71, 76, 83, and 127 mV lower than those of CDs0.2-MnO2 (393 mV), MnO2 (414 mV), CDs0.25-MnO2 (419 mV), CDs0.1-MnO2 (426 mV), and CDs (470 mV) (Fig.S4) respectively, indicating the outstanding OER activity [44,45]. Besides, for further confirming its high OER activity, we have also made a comparison with some previously reported works. As seen in Table S1, the OER activity of such CDs0.15-MnO2 is comparable or superior to those of earth-abundant non-noble metal catalysts, further confirming the excellent OER activity and demonstrating the crucial role of CDs played in determining the ultimate OER activity.

Meanwhile, for evaluating their reaction kinetics, the Tafel slopes of these five types of catalysts have also been calculated based on the LSV with scan rate of 1 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH solution. As shown in Fig. 5c, the optimal CDs0.15-MnO2 displays a relatively low Tafel slope of 43.6 mV dec−1, which is considerably lower than those for CDs0.2-MnO2 (45.3 mV dec−1), MnO2 (49.6 mV dec−1), CDs0.25-MnO2 (57.3 mV dec−1), and CDs0.1-MnO2 (64.5 mV dec−1), respectively, indicating a more favorable OER kinetics and the fastest reaction kinetic of CDs0.15-MnO2 catalysts [46], which is also an important evidence confirming the high performances for OER. Moreover, to better understand the reason for the great enhancement in OER performances, the electrochemically surface areas (ECSA) of different catalysts based on double layer-capacitance (Cdl) have also been calculated. Therefore, the CVs of different catalysts in an appropriate potential range with the scan rate from 20 to 400 mV s−1 have been conducted (Fig.S5). As seen in Fig.S6, the Cdl for CDs0.15-MnO2 is 11.9 mF cm−2, and thus its ECSA is calculated to be 297.5 m2 g−1, which is almost 1.8 times higher than that of MnO2 nanoflowers, suggesting the high surface areas of CDs0.15-MnO2 [47,48]. This is due to the incorporation of CDs can effectively split the MnO2 nanoflowers and thus exposing more surface areas, being consistent with the analyses of TEM. Besides the ECSA, the charge transfer property is also another significant parameter determining the ultimate OER performances of catalysts. In this regard, the charge transfer properties of these catalysts are evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As seen in Fig.S7, the CDs-MnO2 displayed obviously the smaller semicircular diameter in comparison with the MnO2 catalyst, revealing that the CDs-MnO2 possessed smaller charge transfer impedance and better conductivity as compared to MnO2 catalyst. Moreover, among the CDs-MnO2 catalysts, the CDs0.15-MnO2 showed the smallest semicircular diameter, indicating the smallest charge transfer impedance and the best conductivity, which is also consistent with the OER activity.

Apart from the OER activity and kinetic for catalysts, the long-term stability is also crucial for the practical application of catalysts [49]. Accordingly, the long-term CVs and prolonged CP tests have also been performed to evaluate their long-term stability. For comparison, the long-term stability of MnO2 has also been evaluated thoroughly. From Fig. 6 a, we can observe that the polarization curve of MnO2 after 3000 cycles CV display obvious shift to higher voltage in comparison with the initial one, indicating the poor stability toward OER. With regard to CDs0.15-MnO2, as displayed in Fig. 6b, after the successful incorporation of appropriate amount of CDs, the LSV polarization curve of CDs0.15-MnO2 after continuous 3000 cycles CV is almost overlapped with the original one, indicating the excellent long-term stability of CDs0.15-MnO2 and confirming the crucial effects of CDs for the enhancement of stability. Moreover, the prolonged CP tests for 36 h have also been performed to further evaluate their stability. As seen in Fig. 6c, the potential of MnO2 nanoflowers at current density of 10 mA cm−2 display obvious shift to the higher potential, while the potential of CDs0.15-MnO2 is almost constant and displaying negligible morphology variation (Fig.S8), which is also another strong evidence confirming the better stability, showing a promising class of electrocatalysts for OER [50].

In summary, a simple and facile method has been developed for the construction of a novel class of hybrid CDs-MnO2 catalysts. Systematic experiments and characterizations revealed that the introduction of CDs could effectively incorporate into MnO2 nanoflowers, which not only enhanced the conductivity and whole hybrid but also provided more surface areas, leading to more accessible surface active sites and great promotion of OER activity. More importantly, the successful introduction of CDs was also favorable for providing fast charge transfer pathways and avoiding the catalyst from agglomeration or corrosion in alkaline media, greatly contributing to the great promotion of long-term stability. This finding manifested a simple method for the massive preparation of CDs and the construction of a novel class of CDs-modified non-noble catalysts for OER.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Jiangsu province key R & D projects of China (BE2016648), Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (16KJA210001), University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (15KJB150028), Dedicated funds for promoting sci-teci nnovation of Xuzhou (KC17080).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.11.041.
